channel.title: Manoj's Daily Essay Picks
channel.description: Five serious articles and essays selected daily across science, ideas, culture, personal growth, politics, business, strategy, and technology.

item.title: We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow. Turns Out, Some Can Fracture.
item.description: A nonelastic liquid was observed cracking like a brittle solid, challenging a basic assumption about how fluids fracture.

item.title: The Stretch of a Soul
item.description: An exploration of biography, philosophy and whether the fullness of a human life can exceed intellectual explanation.

item.title: B-Sides: Albert O. Hirschman’s “The Passions and the Interests”
item.description: Why capitalism was once defended as a way to tame destructive passions—and how prosperity can eventually threaten freedom.

item.title: ‘It Is Comforting to Be Haunted’: How Attitudes to Abortion Have Changed Through the Ages
item.description: A personal and historical essay restoring bodily experience and circumstance to abstract arguments about abortion.

item.title: Inside the Luddite Festival Harnessing Gen Z’s Rage Against Big Tech
item.description: A grassroots experiment in replacing platform dependence with offline events, analog tools and community-owned infrastructure.

item.title: Lore of the Rings
item.description: Valerie Trouet explains how ancient trees preserve precisely dated evidence of climate shifts, cosmic events and turning points in human history.

item.title: China’s AI Boom Is Creating a Different Kind of Entrepreneur
item.description: How Chinese workers are using AI, frugal innovation and one-person businesses to navigate weak growth, limited capital and precarious employment.

item.title: Seeds of War
item.description: An investigation of how war, foreign aid, import dependence and seed policy have deepened Lebanon’s long-running food crisis.

item.title: The Sumerian Roots of Zaha Hadid’s Architecture
item.description: Diana Darke traces Zaha Hadid’s celebrated futuristic forms to Iraqi landscapes, ancient Sumeria and the traditions of Islamic architecture.

item.title: ‘It Makes Your Heart Sing’: Can a Pioneering Project Show That Rewilding Really Works?
item.description: A reported examination of whether biodiversity credits, carbon markets and ecotourism can make large-scale ecological restoration financially viable.

item.title: Is Life Just Different?
item.description: Philip Ball asks whether biological agency can become a rigorous scientific idea without smuggling mysticism or design into biology.

item.title: The Fall and Rise of Screwworm
item.description: How sterile insects defeated a devastating parasite—and how maintenance failures allowed it to return.

item.title: China's Web Novel Platforms Embraced AI. Now They Are Fighting It
item.description: Chinese fiction platforms encouraged AI-assisted production, then discovered that abundance can destroy quality, trust, and reader value.

item.title: Who Do You Think You Are? There Are Two Answers
item.description: A useful distinction between the identity society recognizes and the deeper identity formed by your values, cares, and self-understanding.

item.title: How the World Celebrated America's 200th Birthday
item.description: America's 1976 bicentennial was not merely a domestic celebration but a global soft-power project shaped by foreign participation.

item.title: Skill nostalgia
item.description: The longing for manual skills reflects frustration with deskilled work and a desire for complexity, mastery, community, and meaning.

item.title: The People Who Will Thrive in the AI Age
item.description: In an AI-abundant world, the scarce human differentiator may be the willingness to exert mental effort rather than raw intelligence.

item.title: The Century of Eric Hobsbawm
item.description: An examination of a historian who made the 20th century intelligible for millions while remaining attached to communism.

item.title: What Makes Humans Stupid
item.description: Stupidity is not simply the absence of intelligence; consequential stupidity often requires intelligence organized badly.

item.title: The End of Reading Is Here
item.description: People still decode words, but the habits needed for sustained reading, synthesis, and independent thought are weakening.

item.title: America At 250 & Beyond
item.description: America’s next democratic evolution should combine citizen participation with deliberative institutions that resist populism and capture.

item.title: The Pulse: a new trend, smart model routing
item.description: Teams are routing each AI task to the cheapest model that can do it well enough instead of always using the strongest model.

item.title: Researchers Reveal the Power of ‘Quantum Proofs’
item.description: Some problems appear to require proofs that are themselves quantum objects, not ordinary classical descriptions.

item.title: When The Machines Deserve Our Consideration
item.description: We may never get definitive proof that AI systems are conscious, but we still need a practical standard for moral consideration.

item.title: How Amsterdam invented the fire department
item.description: Seventeenth-century Amsterdam reduced catastrophic fire damage through technology, organization, incentives, and civic capacity.

item.title: Semiquincentennial Blues
item.description: An essay on America’s 250th birthday, race, patriotic performance, national mythology, and political exhaustion.

item.title: The Meaning of Alienage for Wong Kim Ark
item.description: The stakes of birthright citizenship were daily rights, mobility, legal status, and the vulnerability of Chinese immigrants and their U.S.-born children.

item.title: For the First Time, a Cell Built From Scratch Grows and Divides
item.description: Synthetic biologists assembled nonliving components inside a membrane and produced a cell-like system that grew, copied DNA, and divided.

item.title: We Need A Way To Prove Personhood Online
item.description: AI agents are breaking the old internet assumption that most online actions trace back to an accountable person.

item.title: The war against ‘woke’ could end US science as we know it
item.description: A proposed OMB rule would give political appointees broad authority over research grants, collaborations, conferences, publishing costs, and alignment with presidential priorities.

item.title: Albanians Embrace Their ‘Flamingo Revolution’
item.description: Mass protests in Albania are challenging a luxury development project in the Vjosa-Narta Protected Area.

item.title: Searching for the Unexpected
item.description: A conversation with Carlo Ginzburg about microhistory, marginal voices, archives, chance, and intellectual surprise.
